summary: The Start button and the widget display use two different "next task" computations that can permanently diverge. The widget (StartNextTaskWidget) computes its displayed next task locally from scheduleResult PLUS a live double-check against current task data, so after completing task A it correctly shows task B. But pressing Start calls useTaskStore.startNextTask(), which ignores the displayed task and reads the cached useSchedulerStore.nextScheduledItem — a value derived from the (possibly stale) scheduleResult with NO live double-check. The schedule only recomputes when the store connector's haveTasksChanged() detects a change, and its comparison key (createTaskComparisonKey) omits overallStatus and completedAt. Completing a task that has asyncWaitTime > 0 sets {completed: false, overallStatus: Waiting} — a change that is invisible to the comparison key — so the schedule never recomputes, nextScheduledItem stays pointed at task A indefinitely (until refresh), and Start runs startWorkOnTask(A), which creates a work session against A and writes overallStatus: InProgress to the database — exactly the reported "logged work on the previous task and set its status back to in progress." (For plain completed=true tasks the key does change and the system converges within ~debounce+DB-latency, leaving only a brief race window; the persistent "must refresh" flavor is the async-wait/Waiting path, plus any other status-only transition.) The success toast even shows the displayed task's name (nextTask.title) regardless of which task was actually started, reinforcing the user's belief they started B.
rootCause: Two-part root cause. (1) Stale cached next-task id: useTaskStore.startNextTask() reads useSchedulerStore.getState().nextScheduledItem (src/renderer/store/useTaskStore.ts:1697) and starts it (lines 1704-1708), while the widget displays a DIFFERENT, locally computed nextTask (src/renderer/components/status/StartNextTaskWidget.tsx:92-171) whose live double-check (lines 121-133, comment: "This catches the case where scheduleResult is stale but task arrays are current") filters out completed/Waiting tasks; extractNextScheduledItem (src/renderer/store/useSchedulerStore.ts:236-328) has no such live check — it trusts the frozen item.completed/item.isWaitingOnAsync flags baked into scheduleResult at compute time (isNonWorkItem, useSchedulerStore.ts:88-110). (2) The recompute that would refresh nextScheduledItem never fires for completion-to-Waiting: completing a task with asyncWaitTime>0 routes through processCompletion which returns finalStatus=Waiting (src/shared/task-completion-processor.ts:52-55), so handleComplete/toggleTaskComplete write {completed: false, overallStatus: Waiting} (StartNextTaskWidget.tsx:217-222; useTaskStore.ts:526-530); the store connector only re-syncs tasks to the scheduler when haveTasksChanged() is true (src/renderer/store/storeConnector.ts:101-112 → useSchedulerStore setInputs full-recompute gate at useSchedulerStore.ts:351-356), and createTaskComparisonKey (src/shared/utils/store-comparison.ts:15-35) omits overallStatus (and completedAt) — completed stays false, so the key is identical and no recompute happens. The accompanying session-removal change takes setInputs' activeWorkSessions-only optimization path (useSchedulerStore.ts:412-428), which re-extracts nextScheduledItem from the STALE scheduleResult where A is still the earliest non-completed work item → nextScheduledItem = A, forever (nothing periodically recomputes; recomputeSchedule is only invoked on sprint-mode toggle, useTaskStore.ts:590, and would read the scheduler store's equally stale task copies anyway). The "set back to in progress" side effect is startWorkOnTask writing overallStatus: TaskStatus.InProgress to the DB (useTaskStore.ts:786-788) after creating a work session keyed to the stale taskId (src/renderer/services/workTrackingService.ts:114-149). The misleading toast is StartNextTaskWidget.tsx:181 showing the displayed nextTask.title, not the started task.

files.detail: startNextTask (1689-1724) reads cached useSchedulerStore nextScheduledItem at :1697 and starts it at :1704-1708 with no live-data validation; startWorkOnTask sets overallStatus: InProgress at :786-788 (the status flip); handleComplete-adjacent toggleTaskComplete writes Waiting status at :526-530.

Displayed nextTask computed locally with live double-check at :92-171 (filter at :121-133); handleStart (:177-187) discards it and calls startNextTask() with no argument at :180; toast at :181 names the displayed task regardless of what was started; handleComplete writes {completed:false, overallStatus:Waiting} for async-wait tasks at :217-222.

extractNextScheduledItem (:236-328) computes nextScheduledItem from frozen scheduleResult flags with no live task/step double-check; setInputs full-recompute gate at :351-356 requires inputs.tasks; activeWorkSessions-only optimization path at :412-428 re-extracts from the stale scheduleResult.

createTaskComparisonKey (:15-35) omits overallStatus and completedAt, so a NotStarted/InProgress→Waiting transition with completed unchanged makes haveTasksChanged (:73-85) return false → connector never re-syncs tasks → no schedule recompute.
Debounced task-store subscription (:73-169); only sets changes.tasks when haveTasksChanged is true (:101-112); the optimization-path trigger for sessions-only changes is :133-135 + :147-149.
processCompletion returns finalStatus=Waiting (completed:false downstream) for tasks with asyncWaitTime>0 (:52-55) — the transition invisible to the comparison key.
Confirms a recompute WOULD fix it: Waiting tasks get isWaitingOnAsync=true at :259-267, which isNonWorkItem filters — the staleness is purely the missing recompute trigger.
startWorkSession (:58-172) creates a fresh DB work session keyed to the passed taskId (:114-149) — work logs against whatever stale id startNextTask supplies.

proposedFix: Two complementary changes, both small and behavior-preserving for correct cases. (1) Fix the missing recompute trigger: add `task.overallStatus ?? 'null'` (and `task.completedAt?.toString() ?? 'null'`) to the props array in createTaskComparisonKey (src/shared/utils/store-comparison.ts:17-32). This makes the →Waiting transition (and timer-expiry Waiting→InProgress/Completed transitions) visible to haveTasksChanged, so the store connector re-syncs tasks and setInputs takes the full-recompute path; the fresh schedule marks the task isWaitingOnAsync and extractNextScheduledItem correctly yields task B. Safe because the key is used only for change detection — adding fields can only cause additional (idempotent) recomputes, never fewer. (2) Close the display/action divergence structurally: change startNextTask (src/renderer/store/useTaskStore.ts:1689) to accept an optional explicit item `{ id, type, workflowId? }` and have handleStart (StartNextTaskWidget.tsx:177-187) pass the widget's displayed `nextTask` (it already has id/type/workflow info); fall back to nextScheduledItem only when no item is passed, and in that fallback validate the item against live store data (reject if the live task is completed/Waiting or the live step is completed/waiting/skipped) instead of blindly starting it. startNextTask has exactly one production caller (the widget), so the signature change is low-risk. Fix (1) alone heals the Gantt/scheduler staleness for all consumers; fix (2) alone guarantees the Start button can never act on a different task than displayed — do both.
testPlan: Vitest (run via mcp__diagnostic__run_tests). (a) Regression in src/shared/utils/__tests__/store-comparison.test.ts: two task arrays identical except one task's overallStatus changes NotStarted→Waiting (completed false in both) ⇒ haveTasksChanged returns true — this currently fails, proving the gap (no existing test covers status-only changes). (b) Store-level regression (extend src/renderer/store/__tests__/storeConnector.test.ts or a new useSchedulerStore test): seed the scheduler store with a computed scheduleResult containing tasks A and B; flip A to overallStatus=Waiting in the task store; after the connector debounce, assert useSchedulerStore.getState().nextScheduledItem?.id === B (currently stays A). (c) Widget-level regression in src/renderer/components/status/WorkStatusWidget.startNext.test.tsx: mock the scheduler store with stale nextScheduledItem=A while live task data marks A Waiting (widget displays B); click Start; assert the start action is invoked with B's id (currently startNextTask is invoked argument-less and would start A).
adjacentFindings: Duplicate divergent 'next task' computations violate the project's ONE-implementation rule: StartNextTaskWidget.tsx:92-171 (with live double-check) vs extractNextScheduledItem in useSchedulerStore.ts:236-328 (without). They have already drifted; this bug is the direct consequence. Consider extracting one shared pure function.

Misleading success toast: StartNextTaskWidget.tsx:181 shows `Started: ${nextTask.title}` (the displayed task) even when startNextTask actually started a different cached task — it actively confirms the wrong task to the user.

Even for plain completed=true tasks there is a short race window: the connector debounce (16ms, scheduling.ts:8) plus the tRPC round-trip in updateTask means setInputs' activeWorkSessions-only optimization path (useSchedulerStore.ts:412-428) can briefly re-extract nextScheduledItem=A from the stale scheduleResult before the full recompute lands; passing the displayed task explicitly (fix 2) eliminates this too.

createTaskComparisonKey also omits completedAt, so editing only a Waiting task's completedAt would not trigger recompute even though the scheduler derives the wait-block timing from it (scheduler-converters.ts:263-265).

recomputeSchedule (useSchedulerStore.ts:457-480) reads the scheduler store's own stale tasks/sequencedTasks copies, so even a manual recompute cannot heal staleness caused by a missed connector sync — the scheduler store's task mirrors are only as fresh as haveTasksChanged allows.

Hardcoded status string 'in_progress' in startWorkOnStep DB update (useTaskStore.ts:721-724) instead of the StepStatus enum — violates the 'zero hardcoded strings' standard used elsewhere in the same file (startWorkOnTask uses TaskStatus.InProgress).
WorkStatusWidget.startNext.test.tsx mocks startNextTask entirely (line 22), so no existing test exercises the display-vs-action consistency this bug lives in.
